skill.name: nature-paper-to-patent
skill.description: Convert research papers, theses, technical reports, source code, figures, and manuscripts into evidence-grounded Chinese invention patent draft packages.
skill.summary: Draft Chinese patent materials from research sources with evidence mapping, figures, formulas, and validation checks.
skill.author: Yuan1z0825

security_audit.summary: AI review did not confirm malicious intent, prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, or real network activity. Most high-risk static findings are false positives from Markdown backticks, file extensions, SVG text, and patent-flow terminology. The remaining concern is legitimate local script execution and filesystem writes used to validate drafts and build DOCX, SVG, and PNG outputs.

medium_findings.title: Local Script Execution for Package Generation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run validation and package-building commands, and build_patent_package.py invokes bundled Python renderers through subprocess.run. The calls use argument arrays and bundled script paths rather than shell strings, so command injection evidence was not found, but publication should warn users that the skill executes local scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct evidence of subprocess.run and user-facing run commands. Confidence is high for local execution, but lower for exploitability because shell=True is not used and commands target bundled scripts.

medium_findings.title: User-Directed Filesystem Writes and Copies
medium_findings.description: The project initializer and package builder create directories, copy skill files, copy optional paper input, and write generated DOCX, JSON, SVG, PNG, and report files. This is expected for the skill, but users should run it in a chosen workspace because --force and output paths can affect local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit and central to the workflow. I did not find destructive deletion or hidden path traversal, but broad user-chosen paths make this a moderate operational risk.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner reported many weak cryptographic algorithm hits, but reviewed locations contain Markdown file extensions, drafting schema examples, regular expressions, SVG layout code, validation messages, or patent terminology rather than MD5, SHA1, DES, or other cryptographic use. No evidence found of cryptographic operations or security-sensitive hashing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple representative flagged lines were reviewed and match benign text or validation code. Confidence is high, though the static list was large and grouped rather than individually reproduced.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Detections Are Mostly Documentation
low_findings.description: The scanner treated many Markdown backticks in README.md, SKILL.md, static files, and references as Ruby or shell execution. Reviewed examples are code fences, inline filenames, schema examples, or documented commands rather than executable code paths inside those Markdown files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are visibly Markdown documentation. One documented command leads to real local script execution, which is tracked separately as a medium finding.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation or XML Namespaces
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to GitHub attribution links in README.md and an SVG XML namespace string in the renderer. No evidence found of HTTP clients, network requests, credential transfer, or external telemetry.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed URL occurrences are static text or SVG markup, and repository-wide search found no request, socket, curl, or wget execution path.

low_findings.title: Network-Scanning and Reconnaissance Detections Are Terminology Matches
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged terms such as node, source, target, and flowchart validation as reconnaissance or network-scanning indicators. In context, these terms describe patent figures, graph nodes, claim steps, and validation errors, not host discovery or port scanning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code performs local graph and draft validation only. No network APIs or scanning libraries were found in the inspected files.

low_findings.title: Unicode Escape Detection Is Benign Regex Usage
low_findings.description: The Unicode escape findings in audit_claims.py are a regular expression range for Chinese characters. This is not obfuscation and is used to validate claim terminology.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact context is a readable regex for CJK character ranges and placeholders. There is no encoded payload or dynamic decoding logic at that location.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run Without Shell in Bundled Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: build_patent_package.py uses subprocess.run to call bundled Python scripts for figure and DOCX generation. Arguments are passed as lists and no shell string is used, so this is an operational warning rather than confirmed command injection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real and reachable when users build a package. The implementation avoids the strongest injection indicators, so confidence in maliciousness is low while confidence in execution behavior is high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workspace File Creation and Copying
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts create output directories, copy input and skill files, and write generated package artifacts. This is expected behavior, but it should be run only in a controlled project directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is direct and intentional. I did not find hidden deletion, secret reads, or remote transfer in the reviewed implementation.

content.user_title: Draft Chinese Patents from Research Papers
content.value_statement: Turning research papers into patent drafts requires traceability, claim discipline, and formal document structure. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through evidence-grounded Chinese patent drafting with validation scripts and DOCX outputs.
actual_capabilities: Routes drafting tasks by source format, task mode, and invention type using manifest rules.
Maps source text, equations, figures, and code evidence to stable source IDs.
Drafts Chinese claims, specification, abstract, and abstract figure packages from structured patent data.
Renders claim-aligned black-and-white SVG and PNG flowcharts from draft JSON.
Converts supported formulas into editable Office Math in generated DOCX files.
Runs deterministic checks for claim structure, evidence mapping, figure alignment, and quality thresholds.
limitations: It does not provide a patentability, infringement, ownership, or filing-readiness legal opinion.
It depends on accurate source extraction and inventor confirmation for missing facts.
Scanned PDFs may require external OCR before the workflow has reliable text.
Generated DOCX files still require review by a qualified Chinese patent professional.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Research Disclosure Draft
use_cases.description: Convert a thesis chapter or paper into a structured Chinese invention patent draft with source IDs and inventor questions.

use_cases.title: Review Claim Support
use_cases.description: Check whether each claim feature maps to paper text, formulas, figures, or supplementary evidence before professional review.

use_cases.title: Compare a Paper and Patent
use_cases.description: Create a feature table that separates paper-supported, patent-only, and paper-only technical content.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Full Draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the nature-paper-to-patent skill. Read my paper PDF, identify the source format and invention type, then create the staged source map, evidence ledger, Chinese claims, specification, abstract, and abstract figure package.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Claims Only
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to draft only Chinese claims from this technical disclosure. Map every material limitation to source IDs and keep unsupported features outside the formal claims.

prompt_templates.title: Preserve Formulas and Figures
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill for an algorithm-related invention. Preserve source-supported core formulas as editable Office Math, define every symbol, and generate a claim-aligned method flowchart.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Paper Against Patent
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the paper-patent audit mode. Compare the supplied paper and existing patent feature by feature, label support relationships, and list paper-only and patent-only material without drawing legal priority conclusions.

output_examples.input: A paper PDF about a defect detection model with formulas and method figures.
output: Chinese claims with each material feature linked to source IDs.
A specification draft with editable formulas and symbol definitions.
A main method flowchart reused as the abstract figure.
Validation and claim audit reports for review.

output_examples.input: A scanned paper where some equation symbols are unclear.
output: A source map that marks uncertain OCR blocks.
Claim language limited to confirmed technical features.
Specific inventor questions for unclear formulas and missing implementation details.

output_examples.input: A research paper and an existing patent for the same technology area.
output: Labels for paper-supported, paper-only, patent-only, broader, and narrower content.
A caution list for chronology, inventorship, and professional legal review.

best_practices: Provide the full paper, figures, supplementary materials, and inventor notes before asking for formal claims.
Review all source IDs and inventor questions before generating DOCX deliverables.
Run the validation and claim audit scripts in a controlled workspace before relying on generated files.
anti_patterns: Do not ask the skill to invent missing implementation details or ownership facts.
Do not treat validation success as a filing-ready legal opinion.
Do not copy patent-only content into a paper-based draft without source support.

faq.question: Does this skill provide legal advice?
faq.answer: No. It creates drafting aids and review artifacts. A qualified Chinese patent professional must review any filing decision.

faq.question: Can it work with scanned PDFs?
faq.answer: Yes, but unclear OCR text must be marked as uncertain. The skill should not guess missing symbols or formulas.

faq.question: What files can it produce?
faq.answer: It can produce separate Chinese DOCX files, a structured draft JSON file, SVG and PNG figures, and validation reports.

faq.question: How does it handle formulas?
faq.answer: It records source-supported formulas, defines symbols, and renders formal formulas as editable Office Math in DOCX.

faq.question: Can it compare a paper with an existing patent?
faq.answer: Yes. It can map claim features to paper evidence and separate paper-only from patent-only material.

faq.question: Why are source IDs required?
faq.answer: Source IDs keep claims, formulas, figures, and evidence traceable to the original technical disclosure.
